title: evaluate_let_assignment_command_message_with is fully dead (zero callers incl. tests), superseded by the _with_context variant; sibling evaluate_assignment_command_message_with is test-only — both hidden by #[allow(dead_code)]
evidence: message.rs:35 #[allow(dead_code)] on pub fn evaluate_let_assignment_command_message_with (line 36): workspace-wide grep finds only its definition, the re-export in assignment_command.rs:7, and lib.rs:389 (an #[allow(unused_imports)] pub(crate) use). Zero call sites, not even tests. Production uses the superseded-pair sibling assignment_command_runtime/message.rs:23 evaluate_let_assignment_command_message_with_context (live via session_api/bindings.rs:12 alias → cas_session/src/eval.rs:53,65). message.rs:17-18 evaluate_assignment_command_message_with is called only from src/assignment_command_tests.rs:19,32.
why: A superseded implementation pair where both halves are still present is the recurring bug shape in this repo's memory (old handler hijacks/diverges from new); dead solver-facing entry points also inflate the surface audits must clear.
fix: Delete evaluate_let_assignment_command_message_with (message.rs:33-48) and its re-exports (assignment_command.rs:7, lib.rs:389). Move evaluate_assignment_command_message_with under #[cfg(test)] or inline it into assignment_command_tests.rs.
verify_reason: All factual claims verified exactly: evaluate_let_assignment_command_message_with (assignment_command/message.rs:36, #[allow(dead_code)] at :35) has zero call sites workspace-wide — only its definition, the pub use in assignment_command.rs:7, and the #[allow(unused_imports)] pub(crate) use at lib.rs:389. Sibling evaluate_assignment_command_message_with is called only from #[cfg(test)] assignment_command_tests.rs:19,32. The live path is assignment_command_runtime/message.rs:23 _with_context via session_api/bindings.rs:12 alias into cas_session/src/eval.rs:53,65 (also repl_session_runtime/assignment.rs:18). However, severity demotes to P3: the P2 rationale (superseded-pair hijack/divergence risk) does not apply — mod assignment_command is PRIVATE (lib.rs:27) and re-exported only pub(crate), so the dead half is unreachable from any dispatch or external consumer, and both halves delegate to the same core evaluate_let_assignment_command_with and format_assignment_command_output_message (no diverged logic exists). Remaining impact is dead-code hygiene and audit-surface only.
extra_evidence: Correction strengthening the dead verdict but weakening severity: lib.rs:27 declares `mod assignment_command;` (private), so despite the `pub fn` and module-level `pub use`, the function is not crate-public API — deletion cannot break external users. Divergence check: assignment_command_runtime/eval.rs implements evaluate_let_assignment_command_with_context by calling the core evaluate_let_assignment_command_with with an apply_assignment_with_context closure — deliberate core/runtime layering, not a diverged copy; the only duplicated code is the 3-line eval+render+format composition. Production _with_context wrappers have their own tests (assignment_command_runtime_tests.rs:73-100), so the proposed fix (delete message.rs:33-48, prune assignment_command.rs:7 and lib.rs:388-389 entries, move/inline the test-only sibling) is safe with no coverage loss. Non-Rust search (py/sh/js/ts/json/toml) found zero references.

title: run_suite and format_report are dead superseded wrappers (both layers annotated #[allow(dead_code)]); only the *_filtered successors are live
evidence: health_suite_runner.rs:9-12 pub fn run_suite → suite.rs:10-13 run_suite (also #[allow(dead_code)]): grep shows no callers anywhere; live path is run_suite_filtered (suite.rs:16). Same pattern in health_suite_format_report.rs:8-11 format_report → render/basic.rs:8-9 format_report (#[allow(dead_code)]): only referenced by its own pub use in render.rs:5; live path is render/detailed.rs format_report_filtered, called at health_command_eval/status/run.rs:23. session_api/health.rs re-exports the filtered variants only (as run_health_suite_filtered etc.), and even those aliases have zero external consumers.
why: Two parallel report/runner implementations (basic vs detailed) means a new health case or category only needs to update one, and the dead one silently drifts — a maintenance trap when the campaign adds soundness health suites.
fix: Delete run_suite (both layers), format_report (both layers), render/basic.rs if its helpers (push_basic_result_line) have no other user, and the corresponding allow(dead_code) attributes.
verify_reason: Every element verified: run_suite (health_suite_runner.rs:10 and health_suite_runner/suite.rs:11) and format_report (health_suite_format_report.rs:9 and render/basic.rs:9) all carry #[allow(dead_code)] and have zero callers in a repo-wide search covering .rs (tests/benches/examples included), .py/.sh/.toml/.md/.json, macro registries, and re-exports. Both parent modules are private (lib.rs:120,122), so no downstream crate (cas_cli/cas_session/cas_engine/cas_didactic/cas_android_ffi) can reach them; session_api/health.rs:19-23 re-exports only the *_filtered variants, and even those aliases have no external consumers. Live path confirmed: run_suite_filtered and format_report_filtered called at health_command_eval/status/run.rs:21,23. The basic/detailed renderers are already-divergent parallel implementations (push_basic_result_line vs push_detailed_result_line, None vs category header), so the silent-drift maintenance-trap rationale holds; P2 stands.
extra_evidence: push_basic_result_line (lines/basic.rs:5, re-exported lines.rs:5) is used only by the dead render/basic.rs:17, confirming it can be deleted with basic.rs as the proposed fix conditions. render/header.rs::push_report_header is shared with the live detailed renderer and must be kept. Deletion set: health_suite_runner.rs run_suite wrapper, suite.rs run_suite, health_suite_format_report.rs format_report wrapper, render/basic.rs, the pub use at render.rs:5, lines/basic.rs + its re-export at lines.rs:5, and the four allow(dead_code) attributes.

title: Solve-runtime callback tower: 100 functions with 10+ generic callback params across ~7 plumbing layers (max 35 generics / 40 value params), almost all with exactly 1 call site
evidence: Measured crate-wide: 100 fns with >=10 generic type params (34 with >=15, 15 with >=20) in 48 files; 289 #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]. Worst: dispatch_isolation_with_default_kernels_and_default_arithmetic_pow_function_routes_with_state (solve_runtime_flow_isolation_default_routes.rs:15) = 35 generics, 40 value params, and its body is ONLY RefCell-rewrapping (lines 126-147: 18 RefCell::new calls) to forward to the next layer. The isolation chain I traced is 10 layers deep: cas_solver/src/solve_core_runtime.rs:32 -> solve_runtime_recursive_bound_runtime.rs:8 -> solve_runtime_isolation_dispatch_bound_runtime.rs -> ..._reentrant_context_runtime.rs:10 (17 generics) -> ..._context_runtime.rs:11 (17) -> ..._dispatch_runtime.rs:8 (28) -> ..._default_routes.rs:15 (35) -> ..._custom_pow.rs:15 (21) -> ..._route_callbacks.rs:8 (21) -> isolation_dispatch.rs:482 (real logic). solve_runtime_flow_isolation_dispatch_base.rs:7-96 is a 22-param pure verbatim pass-through adding zero behavior. Call-site census over the 124 pub fns in the solve_runtime*/solver_entrypoints/verification_runtime family: 106 have <=1 call site. Family size: 61 solve_runtime*.rs files, 6,146 lines, essentially all parameter plumbing. Parameter inflation is provably redundant: solve_runtime_isolation_dispatch_context_runtime.rs:121-138,172,178 binds the SAME simplify_expr closure to 3 downstream params (simplify_rhs/simplify_linear_expr/simplify_shortcut) and the SAME collect_steps closure to 5 params (collect_linear_item/collect_route_item/collect_pow_item/collect_function_items/collect_negated_item), so the 33-callback bottom layer has only ~12 distinct behaviors.
why: This is the direct extension-cost blocker for the solve universality campaign: every new hook must be threaded through the tower in lockstep (register_blocked_hint, a recent hook, appears in 8 separate plumbing files: recursive_bound_runtime, isolation_dispatch_bound/context/reentrant/runtime, flow_isolation_default_routes, pow_defaults, pow_core). Each capability fix that needs new state (e.g. the periodic-trig or even-power families in the backlog) pays this tax; the 70+ RefCell rewraps also add runtime indirection and the 20-35-type-param monomorphized towers inflate compile time.
fix: Extract-before-abstract, bottom-up, one layer per cycle with 0-delta huella: (1) delete the pure pass-through layer solve_runtime_flow_isolation_dispatch_base.rs (96 lines, forwards all 22 params verbatim to isolation_dispatch.rs); (2) collapse duplicate parameters — the bottom layers should take ONE simplify callback and ONE collect_steps callback instead of 3 and 5 (removes ~8 generic params per layer mechanically); (3) then fold adjacent single-caller layers pairwise (e.g. custom_pow into default_routes) — each fold is a behavior-preserving inline of a single-call-site function; (4) longer term, the crate already has the target abstraction (RuntimeSolveAdapterState trait in solve_runtime_adapter_state_runtime.rs + concrete RuntimeSolveCtx in solve_runtime_types.rs) — the bound_runtime facade proves a trait bound + small hooks struct (solve_reentrant, register_blocked_hint) is sufficient to replace 30+ closures.
verify_reason: Every quantitative claim independently re-measured and confirmed, most exactly: 100 fns with >=10 generic params (34 >=15, 15 >=20) across 48 files; 289 #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]; worst offender dispatch_isolation_with_default_kernels_and_default_arithmetic_pow_function_routes_with_state (solve_runtime_flow_isolation_default_routes.rs:15) has 35 generics + 40 value params and its body is only 18 RefCell::new rewraps plus verbatim forwarding; 61 solve_runtime*.rs files totaling 6,146 lines. The isolation chain was traced hop-by-hop through 9 single-caller layers from cas_solver/src/solve_core_runtime.rs:32 down to the real logic in isolation_dispatch.rs, and each mid-tower function has exactly 1 call site (grep-verified per function). Parameter redundancy confirmed: solve_runtime_isolation_dispatch_context_runtime.rs binds one simplify_expr closure to 3 downstream params (lines 121/123/138) and one collect_steps closure to 5 (lines 122/126/136/172/178). Extension tax confirmed: register_blocked_hint is threaded through exactly 8 solve_runtime plumbing files. Call-site census reproduced within noise (105/130 family pub fns with <=1 call site vs claimed 106/124). The path is live user-input solve dispatch (solve_inner is the recursive core of solve), so P1 for the extension-cost/coupling dimension stands.
extra_evidence: The claim is slightly UNDERSTATED in one respect: solve_runtime_flow_isolation_dispatch_base.rs (the 22-param "pure verbatim pass-through") has ZERO call sites workspace-wide, not one — the live path (solve_runtime_flow_isolation_route_callbacks.rs:125) calls isolation_dispatch::execute_isolation_dispatch_... directly, and dispatch_base is only referenced by a pub use re-export in solve_runtime_flow_isolation.rs:5. It is dead code, so proposed fix step (1) is a trivially safe deletion. Additional 0-caller fns found in the family: solve_runtime_pipeline_preflight_context_runtime.rs (build_solve_preflight_state_with_existing_condition_derivation_with_state), solve_runtime_adapter_state_runtime.rs (simplifier_derive_requires_from_equation, simplifier_infer_implicit_domain, simplifier_prove_positive), solve_runtime_flow_preflight_entry_guards.rs (try_enter_default_equation_cycle_guard_with_context_ref_and_error_with_state). Minor census correction: family pub fn count is 130 (not 124) by my regex; 105 have <=1 call site, 75 exactly 1, 25 have >=2 — qualitatively identical to the claim.

title: Layering violation: lower-level crate hard-codes rule-name strings produced by cas_engine; step keep/drop/collapse decisions ride on a 5-crate stringly contract
evidence: cas_solver_core does NOT depend on cas_engine (Cargo.toml deps: cas_ast, cas_formatter, cas_math only), yet step_rules.rs:9-25 string-matches rule names defined in cas_engine/src/rules/* ("Sum Exponents", "Binomial Expansion", "Distributive Property", "Combine Like Terms", plus Spanish "Conservar derivada/integral/límite residual" produced in cas_engine/src/eval/actions.rs). is_canonicalization_rule_name (line 24) prefix-matches name.starts_with("Canonicalize") and feeds optimize_steps (step_optimization_runtime.rs:56-67), which collapses/drops such steps. The "Conservar límite residual" literal appears in 9 files across 5 crates (cas_engine eval/actions.rs, cas_solver_core step_rules.rs, cas_didactic filter.rs/latex.rs/expr.rs/focused_rule_substeps.rs/rule_dispatch.rs, cas_solver wire/eval/render/steps.rs, cas_cli tests/limit_contract_tests.rs). Additionally step_optimization_runtime.rs:37 and 64-67 gate semantic-noop and step-drop decisions on rule_name == "Sum Exponents"/"Evaluate Numeric Power" plus rendered-content matching description.contains("1^") && contains("-> 1").
why: Renaming or adding a rule in cas_engine silently changes step filtering in cas_solver_core with zero compiler help — the exact failure shape of the known inherited-RED where a 'Canonicalize Reciprocal Sqrt' step vanished (steps_count=0): any engine rule named 'Canonicalize <X>' is auto-classified mechanical and eligible for collapse by step_rules.rs:24, whether or not that was intended (causal link to that RED is plausible, not verified here). For a didactic-completeness campaign this is a latent step-loss family: every new engine rule name is an unchecked contract change.
fix: Bounded, huella-safe: move the rule-name literals into pub const RULE_* strings (or a small RuleName module) in cas_solver_core — the lowest crate in the chain — and make cas_engine/cas_didactic/cas_solver reference those constants when constructing and matching rule names. Grep shows all producers are reachable. A follow-up cycle can add a typed StepCategory/RuleId on Step so classification stops depending on prefix matching, but constants alone already make renames compile-time-checked.
verify_reason: Every quantitative claim measured true: cas_solver_core has no cas_engine dependency (Cargo.toml deps: cas_ast/cas_formatter/cas_math only) yet step_rules.rs:4-43 hard-codes rule-name literals produced in cas_engine (arithmetic.rs:39117 "Sum Exponents", polynomial/expansion.rs:28 "Binomial Expansion", polynomial/mod.rs:90/431, eval/actions.rs:540 "Conservar límite residual"); the literal appears in exactly 9 files across 5 crates as claimed; step_optimization_runtime.rs:37 and 64-67 gate drop-all-steps and per-step-drop decisions on rule_name strings plus rendered-description substring matching; and the prefix classifier (starts_with("Canonicalize")) feeds a live main-pipeline collapse path (cas_engine/orchestrator.rs:29748 → optimize_steps_semantic → step_optimize.rs:88-111 coalesces any low-importance Canonicalize* run). Refutation angles failed: the module placement is documented (ARCHITECTURE_NEXT_LEVEL_CHECKLIST.md:968) but only as a cas_math cleanup, not as a blessed string contract, and tests only lock existing names — new/renamed "Canonicalize*" rules silently become collapse-eligible. Drift already exists: cas_didactic/step_payloads/prepare.rs:56 comments that every Canonicalize* rule is "always keep" while cas_solver_core's is_always_keep_step_rule_name does not match Canonicalize* and its is_canonicalization_rule_name marks them collapse-eligible — two layers hold contradictory assumptions about the same prefix today. P1 is the right severity for a coupling finding: no user-visible wrong answer, but a live latent step-loss family directly relevant to the didactic-completeness campaign, matching the shape of the known inherited-RED (Canonicalize Reciprocal Sqrt step vanishing, steps_count=0). Proposed fix is feasible: cas_engine, cas_didactic and cas_solver all already depend on cas_solver_core, so constants defined there are reachable by all producers/consumers.
extra_evidence: Additional corroboration beyond the claim: (1) cas_engine/Cargo.toml:12 depends on cas_solver_core and cas_engine/src/engine/transform/rule_application.rs:137,391 already call cas_solver_core::step_rules::is_always_keep_step_rule_name(rule.name()) — so the string vocabulary flows both directions across the boundary; (2) the contradictory prepare.rs:56 comment vs step_rules.rs:9-20 is concrete existing drift, not just hypothetical; (3) "Canonicalize Reciprocal Sqrt" (rationalization.rs:764) is simultaneously mapped to a user-visible didactic name in cas_didactic/visible_rule_names.rs:202 AND classified collapse-eligible by the prefix matcher — the exact tension the finding predicts.

title: Four unused dependencies: regex, thiserror, rustc-hash, and a dangling optional rayon
evidence: Cargo.toml lines 17 (thiserror), 19 (regex 1.12.2), 21 (rustc-hash), 23 (rayon optional). Zero grep hits anywhere in crates/cas_engine/{src,tests,benches,examples} for regex/Regex, thiserror/derive(Error), rustc_hash/FxHash, rayon/par_iter (verified with alias patterns). Zero `cfg(feature = "parallel")` occurrences in cas_engine src — the `parallel = ["rayon", "cas_math/parallel"]` feature (line 27, on by default) pulls rayon into cas_engine's dep graph for nothing; only cas_math/src/gcd_zippel_modp.rs actually uses rayon.
why: regex alone is a heavy transitive tree recompiled on every workspace build/test of the 192k-line crate; dead deps also mislead auditors about what the engine can do (e.g. 'it has regex' suggests string-pattern code paths that don't exist). A universality campaign iterates compile cycles constantly.
fix: Delete regex, thiserror, rustc-hash from [dependencies]; change feature to `parallel = ["cas_math/parallel"]` and drop the optional rayon dep. Huella-safe (no code change); verify with cargo build -p cas_engine && cargo test --workspace.
verify_reason: The dead-dependency facts are CONFIRMED at compiler level: RUSTFLAGS='-Wunused_crate_dependencies' cargo check -p cas_engine reports rayon, regex, rustc_hash, and thiserror all unused in crate cas_engine, and grep over src/tests/benches/examples (including FxHash, derive(Error), par_iter alias patterns) finds zero usages; the proposed feature rewiring (parallel = ["cas_math/parallel"], drop optional rayon) is viable since cas_math/parallel = ["rayon"] exists, cas_math/src/gcd_zippel_modp.rs is the workspace's only rayon user, and cas_solver's `parallel = ["cas_engine/parallel"]` forwarding still resolves. However, the claimed impact — heavy regex transitive tree recompiled on every workspace build — is REFUTED: all four crates stay in cas_engine's own build graph via genuinely used edges (regex used by cas_formatter, thiserror by cas_solver_core, rustc-hash 1.1 by cas_math, rayon by cas_math's default-on parallel feature), so deleting the dead declarations removes zero compilation work; cargo also caches deps, so nothing is recompiled per build anyway. The residual value is dependency-graph hygiene/clarity only, which is P3, not P2.
extra_evidence: Compiler lint output (cargo check -p cas_engine, isolated CARGO_TARGET_DIR, exit 0): "warning: extern crate `rayon` is unused in crate `cas_engine`"; same for regex, rustc_hash, thiserror. Bonus finding from the same lint run: "warning: extern crate `regex` is unused in crate `cas_ast`" (crates/cas_ast/Cargo.toml:11 declares regex; no regex hits in cas_ast/src) — include it in the same cleanup. Used-elsewhere proof: regex used in crates/cas_formatter/src/{display_clean,latex_clean,latex_core}.rs; rustc_hash used in crates/cas_math/src/{multipoly_modp,poly_modp_conv,multinomial_expand}.rs; rayon used only in crates/cas_math/src/gcd_zippel_modp.rs; cas_math/Cargo.toml has parallel = ["rayon"] (default on). Corrected fix: delete regex/thiserror/rustc-hash from crates/cas_engine/Cargo.toml [dependencies], drop optional rayon, set parallel = ["cas_math/parallel"]; also delete regex from crates/cas_ast/Cargo.toml. Verify with cargo build -p cas_engine && cargo test --workspace. No files were modified during verification.

title: Six Pythagorean-identity rules are defined but never registered: register_pythagorean_identities() has zero callers, and two of the rules duplicate live rules by the same struct name in another module
evidence: register_pythagorean_identities (line 221) has exactly 1 workspace reference: its own definition. It is the only registration point for SecTanPythagoreanRule (101), CscCotPythagoreanRule (114), TanToSecPythagoreanRule (127), CotToCscPythagoreanRule (140), SecTanMinusOneIdentityRule (156), CscCotMinusOneIdentityRule (169). The LIVE SecTan/CscCot rules are separate structs with identical names in rules/trigonometry/identities/values_rules.rs:190-215 (registered via sum_to_product_rules.rs:165-166, calling the same cas_math support fns). TanToSec/CotToCsc functionality lives as orchestrator shortcuts (orchestrator.rs:5441, 5571). The MinusOne support fns (try_rewrite_sec_tan_minus_one_identity_zero_expr etc.) have no other callers, but CLI check confirms sec(x)^2-tan(x)^2-1 and csc(x)^2-cot(x)^2-1 both still evaluate to 0 via other routes. The file's register() at line 231 (called from simplifier.rs:441) registers only the 3 non-Pythagorean rules.
why: Classic superseded-both-present trap, and this codebase has already been bitten by it (memory: 'widening a collector can hijack another handler'). A dev extending Pythagorean coverage for the universality campaign can edit or 'fix' the dead copy — same struct name, same support fn — and observe no behavior change, or import the wrong struct and silently double-register.
fix: Delete lines ~95-180 (six define_rule! blocks) and register_pythagorean_identities (~221-231) from trig_canonicalization.rs; leave the 3 rules that register() actually installs. Huella-safe: rules were never registered, so scorecard must be 0-delta.
verify_reason: Workspace-wide search (all .rs incl. tests/benches/examples, py, toml, md) confirms register_pythagorean_identities (crates/cas_engine/src/rules/trig_canonicalization.rs:221) has zero callers — only its own definition — and it is the sole registration point for the six define_rule! structs at lines 101-179. The module is plainly declared (rules/mod.rs:21, no include! aliasing); only register() (line 236) is live, called from engine/simplifier.rs:441, registering just the 3 non-Pythagorean rules. The live SecTan/CscCot rules registered at sum_to_product_rules.rs:165-166 resolve through identities.rs:14 `pub use values_rules::{...}` to the SEPARATE same-name structs in values_rules.rs:190-215 — confirmed dup-by-name with diverged metadata (dead copies: TargetKindSet::SUB|ADD + identity-string names; live copies: unrestricted target kinds + prose names), the exact edit-the-wrong-copy trap claimed. CLI sanity check on the release binary confirms all six identity forms still simplify correctly via the live routes (sec²-tan²→1, sec²-tan²-1→0, csc²-cot²-1→0, 1+tan²→1/cos², 1+cot²→1/sin²; TanToSec/CotToCsc support fns live in orchestrator.rs:5441/5571), so deletion is behavior-neutral. P2 stands: pure dead code would be P3, but the same-name/same-support-fn duplication in a codebase with a documented history of exactly this trap justifies P2.
extra_evidence: Corrections/additions to the fix plan: (1) deleting the six rule blocks requires also trimming the now-unused imports at trig_canonicalization.rs:4-12 (try_rewrite_sec_tan_pythagorean_identity_expr, try_rewrite_csc_cot_pythagorean_identity_expr, try_rewrite_tan_to_sec_pythagorean_identity_expr, try_rewrite_cot_to_csc_pythagorean_identity_expr, try_rewrite_sec_tan_minus_one_identity_zero_expr, try_rewrite_csc_cot_minus_one_identity_zero_expr) or the build breaks/warns; (2) PreserveCompositionRule (lines 74-88) is ALSO dead — registered nowhere and its closure unconditionally returns None (no-op even if registered) — include it in the same cleanup; (3) register() is at line 236, not 231; (4) the two MinusOne support fns in cas_math/src/trig_canonicalization_support.rs (lines 923, 964) lose their last callers after deletion and become follow-up dead code in cas_math (they have unit tests there, so either delete fn+tests or leave as tested library surface).

title: mm_gcd/mm_gcd_modp benches were 'disabled' by commenting out their [[bench]] sections, but cargo auto-discovery still compiles them as unrunnable targets
evidence: Cargo.toml:51-58 comments out the [[bench]] sections with 'Disabled: mm_gcd benchmarks are slow and not needed for regular CI'. But `cargo metadata` shows bench targets mm_gcd, mm_gcd_modp AND common still exist (autobenches defaults to true; commenting the section does not disable discovery). Auto-discovered targets get harness=true, while mm_gcd.rs:250 and mm_gcd_modp.rs:215 use criterion_main! — under the libtest harness that main never executes, so the benches CAN NEVER RUN, yet all 508 lines (plus benches/common.rs compiled as its own bogus 'common' bench target) are compiled by cargo test --workspace (which this repo's validation loop runs per MEMORY: 'cargo test --workspace builds examples/benches too').
why: Pays compile cost on every validation cycle for benchmarks that are physically unrunnable; the misleading Cargo.toml comment says they are disabled when they are only broken. Anyone trying to run mm_gcd to evaluate a GCD change gets silent no-op results.
fix: Either delete the two bench files (+ their common.rs import if unused by remaining benches), or add `autobenches = false` to [package] and keep explicit [[bench]] sections for the 3 real benches. Huella-safe.
verify_reason: Reproduced end-to-end. cargo metadata confirms mm_gcd, mm_gcd_modp, and common still exist as auto-discovered bench targets (edition 2021; commenting [[bench]] does not disable discovery). Running `cargo bench -p cas_engine --bench mm_gcd` live yields the predicted silent no-op: "running 0 tests ... test result: ok ... finished in 0.00s", exit 0 — criterion_main! (mm_gcd.rs:250, mm_gcd_modp.rs:215, verified) never executes under the auto-assigned libtest harness. The developer trap is worse than claimed: docs/ZIPPEL_GCD.md, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:4432, and README.md:70 actively instruct running these benches, and the documented `-- --noplot` form errors on an unrecognized libtest option. Line count (508) and file facts all check out. One evidence detail is wrong (see extra_evidence) but it does not change the defect or severity: P2 stands.
extra_evidence: Correction to the evidence: `cargo test --workspace` does NOT compile these bench targets — verified empirically via `cargo test -p cas_engine --no-run --message-format=json`, which built lib + 69 test targets + 1 example but zero bench targets (bench targets resolve test=false in cargo metadata). The real recurring compile cost is `scripts/ci.sh:113` (`cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`), which check-compiles all three bogus targets on every CI run, plus any unfiltered `cargo bench`. Fix caveat: benches/common.rs is `mod common;`-included by the three live benches (simplification_bench.rs:1, profile_cache.rs:1, repl_end_to_end.rs:4) so the file must stay; the proposed `autobenches = false` + explicit [[bench]] sections is the correct fix and also removes the bogus auto-discovered 'common' bench target. If keeping mm_gcd/mm_gcd_modp files, restore their [[bench]] harness=false sections (docs actively reference running them); if deleting, also update docs/ZIPPEL_GCD.md, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:4432, README.md:70.

title: RuleProfiler::record_domain_assumption is never called, so the health report's 'Domain assumptions used' section is permanently unreachable
evidence: record_domain_assumption (profiler.rs:116-123) has zero callers in the entire workspace (1 grep hit = definition). It is the ONLY code that increments domain_assumption_hits (fetch_add at line 123; lines 151/237 merely aggregate/copy, 390/404 reset). The report builder at lines 307-324 filters `*c > 0` and gates on `total_domain_hits > 0`, so the 'Domain assumptions used:' section can never render.
why: This is dead observability that actively lies during soundness audits: a health report showing no domain-assumption section suggests rules made no domain assumptions, when in fact the channel was never wired. The repo's audit workflow leans on profiler/health reports (health_smoke_tests, scorecards), and domain assumptions are exactly what past P0s hinged on.
fix: Either wire the call where Rewrite domain assumptions are committed (engine/transform records rule applications; add the hook where assumption events are emitted), or delete record_domain_assumption plus the domain_assumption_hits field, its aggregation (151, 237), reset (390, 404), and report section (307-324). Deleting is huella-safe; wiring changes report text only when profiling is on.

title: Trig/hyperbolic power integrands are hand-enumerated per degree (58 functions for tan/cot/sec/csc at degrees 3,4,5,6,8) instead of one parameterized reduction; degree 7 and >=9 silently decline
evidence: grep counts 58 fns matching trig_{tan,cot,sec,csc}_{third..eighth} (degrees 3,4,5,6,8 present; seventh/ninth/tenth absent for these four). trig_tan_fourth/sixth/eighth_affine_antiderivative (2527/2555/2583) are byte-identical except the integer literal and the called _from_parts builder; trig_tan_sixth_antiderivative_from_parts (3178-3208) is a hand-expanded closed form (tan^5/5a - tan^3/3a + tan/a - x). Each degree also has: a pub is_*_target predicate (5807-6027, near-identical bodies), per-degree required_nonzero collectors registered one-by-one (sec_fourth/sixth/eighth_required_nonzero etc. in the table at 21633), and per-degree dispatcher arms in integrate_symbolic_expr (22893-23137). Hyperbolic mirror: is_affine_hyperbolic_square/cubic/fifth/seventh_target (2828-2869).
why: This is the architecture that keeps generating the audit-recurring 'covers the named/odd degree, misses the sibling' wrong-answer/decline families (see 2026-06-30/07-01 audit memory). Every new degree is a ~8-site lockstep edit (builder + affine + quotient + predicate + 2 dispatcher arms + collector + engine residual counterpart), so coverage grows by enumeration and always has holes (7 is missing while 8 exists). Hand-expanded formulas are also a per-degree wrong-answer risk with no shared proof.
fix: Extract-before-abstract: add ONE recursive reduction builder per function family (int tan^n = tan^(n-1)/((n-1)a) - int tan^(n-2), same for cot; sec/csc via the standard by-parts reduction) behind a single degree-parameterized target predicate and one collector; keep the existing enumerated functions as thin delegates first (0-delta huella), then retire them. Start with tan/cot (pure, no by-parts) as a bounded cycle.
verify_reason: Every quantitative/structural claim verified by direct measurement: exactly 58 hand-enumerated trig_{tan,cot,sec,csc} power functions (degrees 3,4,5,6,8; no 7th/9th/10th), byte-identical affine wrappers differing only in the degree literal, hand-expanded closed-form builders, per-degree target predicates (5807-6027), per-degree required_nonzero collectors (21656-21661), per-degree dispatcher arms (22880-23145), and the hyperbolic mirror (2828-2869). Enumerated formulas spot-checked numerically correct. One factual overreach in the claim requires adjustment: bare tan(x)^7/9 and cot(x)^7/9 do NOT decline (a separate sin/cos odd-monomial path catches them), but CLI testing shows the hole map is even more jagged than claimed — tan(2x)^7, tan(3x)^7, tan(2x+1)^7 decline while cot(2x)^7/cot(3x)^7 and tan(2x+1)^6/8 succeed; sec/csc^7/9 decline everywhere; all four decline at even degrees >=10. That asymmetric sibling coverage is precisely the recurring audit failure shape the claim cites, so the architecture finding stands at P1 with a corrected evidence statement.
extra_evidence: Correction to the hole map (tested against target/release/cas_cli, clean tree): bare odd tan/cot powers 7 and 9 succeed via trig_odd_power_affine_antiderivative -> extract_sin_cos_power_monomial path (symbolic_integration_support.rs:4773, Sin|Cos only), so 'degree 7 silently declines' is wrong for bare tan/cot. Actual verified holes: integrate(tan(2x)^7), tan(3x)^7, tan(2x+1)^7 all return unevaluated residual while cot(2x)^7/cot(3x)^7 succeed (asymmetric tan/cot sibling hole); sec(x)^7, sec(3x)^7, csc(x)^7, sec/csc^9 decline everywhere; tan/cot/sec/csc^10 decline; tan(x/2)^7 succeeds but triggers depth_overflow simplifier warnings. All failures are honest residuals, not wrong answers (tan(2x+1)^6, sec(3x)^8, csc(2x)^8 outputs verified by numeric differentiation), so impact is capability+maintenance, not soundness. Proposed fix direction (parameterized recursive reduction, tan/cot first) remains valid; note it must also subsume the affine-argument case since that is where the current holes actually live.

title: solve_runtime_flow_* callback towers: top function takes 41 parameters (35 generic callback type params); ~20 functions >=10 params across 36 files
evidence: Measured with a param-counting script over src/solve_runtime_flow*.rs (36 files, 43 fns, 3,494 lines): dispatch_isolation_with_default_kernels_and_default_arithmetic_pow_function_routes_with_state = 41 params / 35 generics (isolation_default_routes.rs:15); apply_pow_isolation_with_default_runtime_config_and_unified_step_mapper_for_var_with_state = 31 params (isolation_pow_defaults.rs:11); apply_strategy_kind_with_default_kernels_and_state = 26 (strategy_dispatch_apply_core.rs:16); 17 more fns at 14-28 params. Read the 41-param signature: every param is a hook (FSimplifyRhs, FProveNonzeroStatus, FMapPowEnsureError, ...) threaded verbatim to the next wrapper; whole files exist only to plug one default and forward the rest.
why: Direct blocker for extending the solver (a campaign front): adding one capability that needs a new hook means threading a parameter through every wrapper in the chain (shotgun surgery), and the function names encode the wiring (e.g. ..._and_default_arithmetic_routes_and_custom_pow_function_with_state), so each variant is a new file. NOTE: this is in cas_solver_core, outside the cas_math file scope, measured because the dimension brief explicitly requested it.
fix: Introduce an IsolationHooks/SolveFlowHooks context struct (fields = the FnMut hooks, constructor provides the current defaults) and convert the innermost dispatch first, keeping the existing 43 signatures as thin adapters that build the struct (behavior-preserving, 0-delta huella); collapse wrapper files as call sites migrate.
verify_reason: Every quantitative claim reproduced by independent measurement: 36 files / 43 fns / 3,494 lines exact; headline fn at solve_runtime_flow_isolation_default_routes.rs:15 measures 41 params / 35 generics with the same counting method (true 40/34, trailing-comma artifact, immaterial); 31-param and 26-param fns confirmed at the claimed locations. Read the full headline signature: 30 generic params are FnMut hook types, and the body is pure RefCell-wrap-and-forward to the next wrapper, confirming the shotgun-surgery mechanic. Liveness confirmed: the tower is the real solve path — flow fns → solve_runtime_isolation_dispatch_runtime/orchestration_context_runtime → solve_runtime_recursive_bound_runtime → crates/cas_engine/src/solve_core_runtime.rs::solve_inner, wired at crates/cas_engine/src/api.rs:114 and :215 (mirrored in crates/cas_solver/src/solve_core_runtime.rs). P1 stands: on the live dispatch path of every solve() call, direct blocker for adding solver hooks, clippy::too_many_arguments suppressed throughout rather than addressed.
extra_evidence: Corrected counts: headline fn is 40 params / 34 generics when trailing comma is excluded (both auditor and verifier scripts count 41/35); functions >=10 params = 29, >=14 params = 21 — worse than claimed. Full top-of-list: 41, 31, 28, 27, 26, 26, 23, 23, 23, 21, 20, 19x4, 18, 17x2, 15, 14x2 params. Caller chain proving liveness: crates/cas_engine/src/api.rs:114,215 → crates/cas_engine/src/solve_core_runtime.rs → crates/cas_solver_core/src/solve_runtime_recursive_bound_runtime.rs (solve_inner_with_runtime_state_and_default_recursive_routes) → solve_runtime_orchestration_context_runtime.rs / solve_runtime_isolation_dispatch_runtime.rs → the solve_runtime_flow* tower. The proposed hooks-struct fix is consistent with the observed structure (all hooks share the &mut T state-threading convention, so a struct of FnMut fields is mechanically derivable).

title: server_colab.py is a rotted near-clone of server.py that cannot even start (verified crash) and whose domain coercion returns None

verify_reason: All claims reproduced by execution: (1) main() crashes with TypeError: env_int() got multiple values for argument 'default' — app_config.env_int(name, default) vs the 3-arg call at web/server_colab.py:473; (2) _coerce_domain_mode('generic') and ('strict') return None (missing final `return value` present in server.py:220), and the frontend (web/index.html:1899) always sends a non-empty select value, so every eval would build ['--domain', None]; (3) dead unreachable `return value` at line 105 confirmed; (4) AST diff exactly matches: 12 shared top-level functions, 8 diverged bodies including _merge_equiv_with_derive_steps (colab lacks server.py's wire-message rebuild, server.py:122-146) and no --lang support (server.py:902); (5) zero tests start either server (test_web_examples_smoke.py invokes cas_cli directly); (6) not deliberate layering — docs/ENGINE_COMBINATION_LEDGER.md:2513 logs server_colab.py parity as 'pending', i.e., acknowledged rot. Per the dup protocol, diverged copies with different behavior (two being verified outright bugs) sustains P1.
extra_evidence: Executed repro: `_coerce_domain_mode('generic')` -> None, `_coerce_domain_mode('')` -> 'generic' (only the empty/default path works); `server_colab.main()` -> TypeError immediately. The file's own docstring (line 13) tells pm2 users to launch web/server.py, not server_colab.py, so the documented Colab path never exercises this file's fixes. Only repo references to server_colab are ledger doc entries listing it in cohesion scope of past web work, confirming the double-maintenance cost is real (parity work has been attempted and logged as pending). Proposed fix (extract web/eval_common.py or delete the file) is consistent with the evidence; app_config.py already demonstrates the shared-module import pattern works for both servers.

title: Server binds all interfaces with wildcard CORS, no auth, unbounded request bodies, and a single-threaded request loop driving 8-120s subprocesses
evidence: Line 994: `http.server.HTTPServer(('', PORT), ...)` — '' binds 0.0.0.0 (LAN-reachable). Lines 962/969/976: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on every JSON response plus permissive OPTIONS, so any website in the user's browser can POST /api/eval, /api/clear, /api/import, /api/delete-* (no auth, session_id is client-chosen). Lines 403, 417, 519, 543, 564: `self.rfile.read(int(headers['Content-Length']))` with no cap — one request can buffer GBs. HTTPServer is non-threading and call_cas_cli allows up to 60-120s per subprocess (line 269), so one slow expression blocks every other client; handle_import (line 492) spawns one CLI subprocess per imported function with no count cap (amplification). Also `send_json_error(str(e))` on bare Exception (686) and raw `result.stderr` (928) leak internals to the client.
why: The scope names security as the top concern for this file. As-is, any machine on the same network (or any web page, via the open CORS) gets free arbitrary-expression compute, session tampering/deletion, and a trivial single-request denial of service against the didactic web UI used to validate engine work.
fix: Bounded, behavior-preserving for local use: default bind to 127.0.0.1 with an env opt-in for 0.0.0.0; drop the ACAO:* headers (the UI is served same-origin so CORS is unnecessary) or reflect only an allowlisted origin; cap Content-Length (e.g. 2 MB -> 413); switch to ThreadingHTTPServer (per-request handler instances make existing state safe); replace str(e)/stderr passthrough with a generic message + server-side log.
verify_reason: Every factual element checks out by direct read of /Users/javiergimenezmoya/developer/math/web/server.py. (1) Line 994: `http.server.HTTPServer(('', PORT), CASHandler)` — empty host = INADDR_ANY, LAN-reachable; PORT defaults 8080 (line 39). (2) Lines 962/969/976: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on send_json, send_json_error, and do_OPTIONS (which also allows POST + Content-Type header, so even preflighted cross-origin JSON POSTs succeed); handlers json.loads the body regardless of Content-Type, so a no-preflight text/plain POST from any web page also executes. (3) Unbounded `self.rfile.read(int(Content-Length))` confirmed at 403-404, 417-418, 519-520, 543-544, 564-565 — no cap anywhere (grep for MAX/413 finds nothing). (4) Plain non-threading HTTPServer + serve_forever (1011); no ThreadingMixIn/ThreadingHTTPServer in the file; `_compute_subprocess_timeout_seconds` (line 268-269) allows 60s, 120s for >10000-char expressions, so one request blocks all clients for up to 2 minutes. (5) handle_import calls eval_and_store (one cas_cli subprocess each) per imported function in a loop (lines 485-504) with no count cap. (6) Info leaks confirmed: bare `except Exception as e: self.send_json_error(str(e))` at 685-686 (and 515-516, 536-537), and raw `result.stderr` returned to the client at line 927-928. (7) No auth: do_POST (357-366) routes /api/eval, /api/clear, /api/import, /api/delete-function, /api/delete-variable directly; session_id is client-chosen with default 'default' (e.g. line 524), so any origin/LAN peer can tamper with or clear the shared default session. Trivially triggerable by any LAN host or any web page in the developer's browser. No RCE (subprocess uses arg-list + stdin, no shell; session paths are sha256-hashed so no traversal), which matches the claimed P1 rather than P0. Proposed fix (bind 127.0.0.1 by default, drop ACAO:*, cap Content-Length, ThreadingHTTPServer, generic error messages) is sound and behavior-preserving for local use.
extra_evidence: Two minor corrections that don't change the verdict: the evidence cites "line 269" for the timeout — the 60/120s values are at line 269 inside `_compute_subprocess_timeout_seconds` starting line 268 (accurate); and handle_import's per-function subprocess loop is at lines 485-504 with the eval_and_store call at 492 (accurate). Additional supporting detail beyond the claim: `_session_snapshot_path` (line 153) sha256-hashes session_id, so there is no path-traversal escalation, but the unauthenticated client-chosen session_id still allows cross-origin tampering with any known/default session. Also, do_OPTIONS explicitly whitelists Content-Type, meaning even strict preflighting browsers permit the cross-origin JSON POSTs.

title: Exact linear-surd sign algorithm implemented 3x across 3 crates, with capability drift between copies
evidence: Same algorithm (sign(a+b*sqrt(n)) via same-sign shortcut then sign(b)*sign(b^2*n-a^2)) at: (1) cas_math/src/root_forms.rs:1876 `provable_sign_vs_zero` (canonical, documented public API); (2) cas_solver_core/src/solution_set.rs:117 `sign_of_linear_surd` (re-implemented on decomposed (p,q,n)); (3) cas_solver/src/solve_backend_local.rs:28 `linear_surd_sign` — its own doc comment admits 'the same exact surd-sign logic as cas_math::root_forms::provable_sign_vs_zero'. Copies have already diverged in capability: solution_set.rs's `as_surd_value` (line 108) additionally recognizes phi=1/2+1/2*sqrt(5) and `sign_of_sum_two_surds` (line 143) decides DISTINCT-radicand sums p+q*sqrt(m)+s*sqrt(n); neither exists in root_forms nor in the solve_backend copy, so e.g. classify_trig_threshold (solve_backend_local.rs:~80) cannot decide a sqrt(2)+sqrt(3)-style threshold that solution_set's comparator could.
why: Memory notes identify the surd-sign helpers as THE chokepoint that closed multiple P0 wrong-answer families ('surd-sign guard upgrade closes P0 families'). Every future decidability upgrade (nth roots, two-surd sums, phi, transcendental bounds) must now be hand-ported to 3 sites or coverage silently diverges — the exact recurring bug shape ('a reduction covers the named case, misses the sibling') the audits keep finding.
fix: Make cas_math::root_forms canonical: expose `pub fn linear_surd_sign(a,b,n) -> Ordering` (the decomposed form both solver copies need), reimplement provable_sign_vs_zero on top of it, and lift `sign_of_sum_two_surds` + the phi extension into root_forms. Delete solve_backend_local::linear_surd_sign and delegate solution_set.rs to the cas_math versions (cas_solver_core already depends on cas_math). Behavior-preserving for existing callers; verify with stash-regenerated huella.
verify_reason: All three copies verified at the claimed locations with the identical algorithm (same-sign shortcut, then sign(b)*sign(b^2*n-a^2)): root_forms.rs:1876 provable_sign_vs_zero, solution_set.rs:117 sign_of_linear_surd, solve_backend_local.rs:28 linear_surd_sign (whose doc comment at line 22 explicitly admits duplicating root_forms). Duplication is NOT deliberate layering: both solver crates depend on cas_math, root_forms is unconditionally public (works under cas_solver_core's default-features=false), and solve_backend_local.rs itself imports the canonical provable_sign_vs_zero at line 392 — the copies exist only because the canonical API lacks a decomposed (a,b,n) entry point. Capability drift verified: solution_set's as_surd_value (line 108) recognizes Constant::Phi and sign_of_sum_two_surds (line 143) decides distinct-radicand sums, while root_forms has neither (its test at 2999-3000 asserts as_linear_surd(sqrt(2)+sqrt(3))==None) and classify_trig_threshold (solve_backend_local.rs:79) uses as_linear_surd directly, so it cannot decide a sqrt(2)+sqrt(3)-style threshold that solution_set's comparator could. P1 stands: the drift yields honest declines today (not wrong answers — the core algorithm is identical in all copies), but these are the memory-documented chokepoint helpers where every decidability upgrade must now be hand-ported to 3 sites.
extra_evidence: Finding understates exposure: linear_surd_sign has additional call sites at solve_backend_local.rs:4836-4838, 4870, and 5057-5058 (interval-endpoint classification), beyond the two inv-trig helpers cited. Also, comments at solve_backend_local.rs:1572 and 1603 already reason about solver_core's as_surd_value capabilities from the other crate — evidence the drift is confusing even to maintainers. Proposed fix feasibility confirmed: cas_math::root_forms is not feature-gated (lib.rs:154), so cas_solver_core (default-features=false) can call a new decomposed pub fn.

title: solve_runtime_flow_* family: 92 functions passing up to 41 params (30 generic callbacks) through 8-layer single-use forwarding chains
evidence: Measured across the 61 solve_runtime*.rs files (6,146 lines): 92 pub fns, 48 with >=10 params, 15 with >=20, max = dispatch_isolation_with_default_kernels_and_default_arithmetic_pow_function_routes_with_state (solve_runtime_flow_isolation_default_routes.rs:15) with 41 params / 30 F-generics. Read solve_runtime_flow_isolation_custom_pow.rs:15-96 in full: 23 params, 17 callback generics, then 7 RefCell::new re-wrappings (65 RefCell wraps across the family) just to forward into the next layer. Call-site counts: each chain layer (route_callbacks -> custom_pow -> default_routes -> dispatch_runtime -> dispatch_context_runtime -> dispatch_bound_runtime) has EXACTLY 1 caller (grep-verified), i.e. pure forwarding. cas_solver_core has 289 #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]. A 17-method trait RuntimeSolveAdapterState already exists (solve_runtime_adapter_state_runtime.rs, impl'd by Simplifier in crates/cas_engine/src/solve_runtime_state_impl.rs) proving the context-struct/trait shape works at the boundary.
why: This is the isolation/strategy dispatch spine of solve(). Extending solve (new route, new hook, threading a flag like Language or a periodicity callback) requires editing every layer's signature + where-clause + closure re-wrapping in lockstep across ~8 files — the exact shotgun surgery a universality campaign on solve will hit repeatedly. The RefCell re-borrow dance also hides panic risk (double borrow) that a struct would eliminate.
fix: Extract-before-abstract, one chain link per cycle: since every *_with_default_* layer has exactly 1 caller, inline/collapse the deepest pair first (route_callbacks + custom_pow) by replacing their fixed 'default kernel' callback bundles with direct calls, keeping only genuinely variable hooks (on_pow_route, on_function_route, map_step, map_unsupported_error) as parameters or as 4 methods added to RuntimeSolveAdapterState. Each collapse is behavior-preserving (0-delta huella). Do NOT redesign the whole chain in one pass.
verify_reason: All quantitative claims independently re-measured and verified, most exactly: 61 files / 6,146 lines; 48 fns >=10 params and 15 fns >=20 (exact); max = 41 params / 30 F-generics at solve_runtime_flow_isolation_default_routes.rs:15 (exact function); 65 RefCell::new wraps family-wide and 7 in custom_pow.rs (exact); 289 too_many_arguments allows (exact); every named chain layer grep-verified to have EXACTLY 1 caller (route_callbacks<-custom_pow:108<-default_routes:148<-dispatch_runtime:137<-context_runtime:113<-reentrant:107<-bound_runtime:65<-recursive_bound_runtime:286), i.e. pure single-use forwarding; RuntimeSolveAdapterState trait has 17 methods and is impl'd by Simplifier at cas_engine/src/solve_runtime_state_impl.rs:4. Hot-path confirmed: public solve() (cas_engine/src/api.rs:104) routes through solve_core_runtime::solve_inner into this chain for every solve call. Discrepancies found only UNDERSTATE the problem (109 pub fns vs 92 claimed) or are trivial (custom_pow has 22 params, not 23). P1 severity stands: this is the solve() dispatch spine and any signature extension (new flag/hook) requires lockstep edits across ~8 files.
extra_evidence: Corrections that strengthen the claim: actual pub fn count is 109 (not 92). One soft spot: the 'RefCell double-borrow panic risk' aside is speculative — RefCells are created fresh per call frame, so recursion through solve_equation_with_var builds new frames and cannot re-borrow an outer frame's cells; no concrete panic path found. This does not affect the coupling verdict. The proposed extract-before-abstract fix is consistent with the verified single-caller topology (deepest pair route_callbacks+custom_pow is safely collapsible with 0 behavior change).

title: Layering loop via stringly-typed rule names: lower crate cas_solver_core hardcodes rule titles defined in cas_engine, and cas_engine/cas_didactic branch on the result
evidence: step_rules.rs:10-20 is_always_keep_step_rule_name matches literals 'Sum Exponents', 'Cancel Exact Additive Pairs', 'Conservar integral residual', etc. Those names are defined by define_rule!/Step construction in the HIGHER crate cas_engine (e.g. 'Cancel Exact Additive Pairs' at crates/cas_engine/src/rules/arithmetic.rs:14250; 'Sum Exponents' at arithmetic.rs:39117; 'Conservar integral residual' created in cas_engine/src/eval/simplify_action.rs:2632 AND cas_solver_core/src/eval_step_pipeline.rs:1230). cas_engine then consumes the classification to decide whether a step is KEPT: engine/transform/rule_application.rs:137 and :391 (is_didactic_rule gate that bypasses semantic-equality step suppression). 'Conservar integral residual' appears 33 times across 4 crates with no shared constant. cas_didactic additionally string-matches rule_name in 36 places across 19 files (step_payloads/build.rs:78-176, focused_rule_substeps.rs:230/6561/7351, enrichment_pipeline/rule_dispatch.rs:21-59, etc.).
why: Renaming (or localizing — the active es/en campaign touches exactly these Spanish titles) a rule silently flips keep/drop and enrichment decisions with zero compile error. There is a documented prior incident of exactly this failure shape (matrix-smoke silently dropping the 'Canonicalize Reciprocal Sqrt' step, steps_count=0). For a completeness campaign on didactic output, this is the top silent-regression channel.
fix: Move the rule-name literals used by step_rules.rs into pub const &str items in cas_solver_core (the lowest common dependency), and reference those consts from the cas_engine define_rule!/Step call sites and cas_didactic matchers. Strings stay byte-identical -> 0-delta huella. Do it family-by-family starting with the three 'Conservar * residual' names and the is_always_keep list (10 names).
verify_reason: Every load-bearing element of the claim verified by direct measurement: (1) cas_solver_core is the lower crate (cas_engine/Cargo.toml:12 depends on it) yet step_rules.rs:9-20 hardcodes 10 rule-name literals defined by define_rule! in cas_engine (arithmetic.rs:14250 'Cancel Exact Additive Pairs', :39117 'Sum Exponents'); (2) rule_application.rs:137 and :391 use is_always_keep_step_rule_name to bypass semantic-equality step suppression, so a rename silently flips keep/drop with no compile error; (3) 'Conservar integral residual' appears exactly 33 times with dual creation sites (simplify_action.rs:2632 and eval_step_pipeline.rs:1230) and zero shared constants workspace-wide; (4) cas_didactic string-matches rule_name in 31+ literal comparisons across 10 src files plus prefix/contains matchers and an es-display mapping keyed on internal names; (5) the active es/en localization campaign and a documented prior silent-step-drop incident (Canonicalize Reciprocal Sqrt, steps_count=0) make the failure scenario concrete, not hypothetical. title: Unbounded integer-power materialization bypasses the MAX_ABS_POW=1000 guard, uninterruptible DoS from trivial input
evidence: try_rewrite_evaluate_power_expr (lines 156-216) handles Number^Number: for an integer base/exponent it computes num_traits::Pow::pow(&outside_rat, pow_num.unsigned_abs()) where pow_num = numer.to_i32() (line 169,175). The ONLY size limit is i32 (~2.1e9). const_eval.rs:19,37 documents MAX_ABS_POW=1000 as the intended chokepoint (try_eval_pow_literal returns None for |exp|>1000), and EvaluatePowerRule (power_rules.rs:534-548) calls that FIRST, then falls through to this unbounded path. Verified live: `./target/debug/cas_cli eval "2^100000000"` runs >30s (timeout, rc=124); `2^1000000` takes 4.22s producing a ~300k-digit number; `2^1000000` with `--time-budget-ms 200` still runs the full 25s (rc=124) — the atomic Pow::pow call is not checkpointed so no CLI/simplifier budget can interrupt it.
why: A universality/completeness campaign on solve/integrate that materializes numeric powers will hang the whole process (and the web server subprocess) on inputs as small as `2^100000000`; it silently defeats the documented MAX_ABS_POW safety limit and every time budget, so any front that folds a large a^n is a latent hang.
fix: Before calling Pow::pow, bound the result magnitude the same way const_eval does — e.g. reject when pow_num.unsigned_abs() exceeds MAX_ABS_POW (reuse the const_eval constant) or when estimated bit-length (base.bits()*exp) exceeds a cap; return None to keep the power symbolic.
verify_reason: Every factual element reproduced. Code: power_rules.rs (crates/cas_engine/src/rules/exponents/power_rules.rs:540-547) tries the MAX_ABS_POW=1000-guarded const_eval path first, then falls through to try_rewrite_evaluate_power_expr (crates/cas_math/src/power_eval_support.rs:169-175), where for integer exponents extract_root_factor(base,1) always yields a nontrivial outside factor, so Pow::pow runs with any i32 exponent — no magnitude guard. Live: 2^1001 fully materializes (guard bypassed at 1001); 2^1000000 takes 4.37s (~301k digits); 2^100000000 pegs a core >30s (rc=124 under external timeout); --time-budget-ms 200 does NOT interrupt (2^1000000 still runs 3.45s to completion; 2^100000000 with --budget small --time-budget-ms 100 still runs the full 30s). User input reaches the site directly via CLI eval and via the web servers' subprocess calls. P1 stands: availability/DoS defeating a documented safety limit and all internal budgets, trivially triggerable; not P0 since it produces no wrong answers and the web layer has a hard subprocess kill.
extra_evidence: Minor scope correction (does not change severity): web/server.py and web/server_colab.py wrap cas_cli in subprocess.run(timeout=...) with TimeoutExpired handled (60s default legacy timeout, server.py:268-276), so the web SERVER survives — each malicious request costs up to ~60s of pegged CPU rather than a permanent server hang. The uninterruptible-hang scenario fully applies to direct CLI use and to audit/workflow agents invoking the CLI without an external kill. Worst case is worse than tested: pow_num can reach i32::MAX (~2.1e9 → ~260MB bigint plus quadratic decimal formatting → effectively unbounded). Fix as proposed: gate pow_num.unsigned_abs() by cas_math::const_eval::MAX_ABS_POW (or an estimated bits cap, base.bits()*|exp|) before the Pow::pow at power_eval_support.rs:175, returning None to stay symbolic — consistent with const_eval's documented contract.

title: /api/import materializes a client-controlled max_ref range: a tiny JSON body causes unbounded memory allocation (remote OOM DoS)
evidence: web/server.py:437-453: `ref = r.get('ref', 0)` ... `max_ref = max(max_ref, ref)` (line 440) then `for i in range(1, max_ref + 1): imported_results.append({...})` (lines 443-453). max_ref comes straight from the request body with no upper bound. The server binds all interfaces (`HTTPServer(('', PORT), ...)`, line 994) with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` (line 962) and no auth. A ~60-byte POST `{"session_id":"x","results":[{"ref":1000000000}]}` allocates ~10^9 dicts (100+ GB).
why: Security matters most for this file per the audit brief; any LAN peer (or any site via the permissive CORS/no-origin-check setup) can crash the dev server with one request, taking down the interactive verification surface used during engine campaigns.
fix: Clamp max_ref (e.g. `max_ref = min(max_ref, len(results) + 1000)` or reject imports where max_ref > len(results)*2), and skip refs above the clamp instead of padding to them.
verify_reason: All load-bearing claims verified in web/server.py. handle_import (lines 414-453) derives max_ref exclusively from client input `r.get('ref', 0)` with only a `ref > 0` filter and NO upper bound (line 440), then materializes it at line 443 `for i in range(1, max_ref + 1): imported_results.append({...})`, appending one ~300-byte dict per iteration. do_POST (357-369) routes /api/import to the handler with no auth. The server binds all interfaces `HTTPServer(('', PORT), ...)` (line 994). Type reachability holds: an attacker sends an integer ref, which passes `ref and ref > 0`, `max()`, and `range()` without error. A ~60-byte body `{"session_id":"x","results":[{"ref":1000000000}]}` forces ~10^9 dict allocations -> OOM. Genuine unauthenticated, remotely-reachable unbounded-allocation DoS.
extra_evidence: Correction to the finding's reasoning (does not lower severity): the permissive CORS (`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`, line 962) is a red herring for THIS DoS — CORS restricts reading the response, not sending the request, so it neither enables nor blocks the attack. The genuine exposure vectors are: (1) all-interface bind at line 994 -> any LAN peer can POST; (2) drive-by web exploitation -> handle_import (line 419) calls `json.loads(body)` without ever checking Content-Type, so a malicious site can issue a simple no-preflight cross-origin POST (e.g. Content-Type: text/plain) to localhost:PORT and trigger it. Note this is a local/dev "ExpliCAS Web REPL" server, but the all-interface bind plus machine-wide impact of a 100+ GB allocation (OOM killer killing unrelated processes) justifies P1 under the file's security-first brief. Proposed fix (clamp max_ref to ~len(results) and skip refs above it) is sound.

title: Deeply-nested input crashes the whole process via unbounded recursive-descent parse (stack overflow / SIGABRT)
evidence: Verified live: `python3 -c "print('('*60000+'1'+')'*60000)" | ./target/release/cas_cli eval --format json` → `thread 'main' has overflowed its stack / fatal runtime error: stack overflow, aborting`, EXIT 134. `grep -rn 'depth|MAX_DEPTH|recursion' crates/cas_parser/src` returns NOTHING — the recursive-descent parser (crates/cas_parser/src/parser.rs, latex_parser.rs) has no recursion-depth guard. 120001 chars is under the web `--max-chars 500000` limit, so the same input reaches the parser through web/server.py's subprocess call and aborts the cas_cli process.
why: A universality/completeness campaign hardens solve/integrate/limits, but any adversarial or accidental deeply-nested string aborts the process before the solver ever runs — the exact 'an equation string typed by a user must never panic the process' invariant. The abort is unrecoverable (not a catchable Result), unlike simplify's MAX_SIMPLIFY_DEPTH guard.
fix: Add a recursion-depth counter to the recursive-descent entry points (parse_expr/parse_primary/parse_atom) returning a parse error past a bound (e.g. a few thousand), mirroring the engine's MAX_SIMPLIFY_DEPTH pattern. Note: file is in cas_parser, outside the nominal cas_solver_core scope, but is the process-crash root the panic-hunt method calls out.
verify_reason: Reproduced live: 60000-deep nested parens → exit 134 'fatal runtime error: stack overflow, aborting'; lldb backtrace pins the overflow inside cas_parser::parser::parse_term (nom recursive descent), and grep confirms zero depth/recursion guards in crates/cas_parser/src. User input reaches it on all surfaces: CLI/REPL directly; web/server.py pipes the user expression to cas_cli with --max-chars 500000 (120k chars passes); and crates/cas_android_ffi (cdylib JNI) calls cas_solver::wire::eval_str_to_wire IN-PROCESS with a catch_unwind net that cannot catch stack-overflow aborts, so on Android this kills the whole app. The repo's own panic_fence.rs (catch_unwind on every REPL command) proves 'user input must never kill the process' is an explicit project invariant that this bug bypasses. P1 severity is appropriate: unrecoverable abort, not a P0 wrong answer.
extra_evidence: Two corrections strengthening the finding: (1) the crash threshold is far lower than the evidence's 60000 — depth 3000 already aborts (depth 1500 succeeds), so ~2-3k nested parens suffice; (2) the highest-impact path is not the web server (web/server.py checks returncode!=0 and degrades gracefully per-request via subprocess isolation) but crates/cas_android_ffi/src/lib.rs, whose in-process JNI evalWire cannot be protected by its documented catch_unwind guard against a stack-overflow abort — an adversarial expression string crashes the host Android app. Proposed fix (depth counter in parse_expr/parse_term/parse_atom returning a nom error past ~500-1000 levels) is the right shape; latex_parser.rs should get the same guard.

title: Exact quadratic-surd sign/comparison kernel implemented 3x across 3 crates, with feature drift already visible between copies

Rewrite solution_set.rs sign_of_linear_surd/as_surd_value/as_nth_root_value and solve_backend_local.rs cmp_rational_to_quadratic_surd/bound_surd/bound_nth_root as thin wrappers. Delete split_linear_surd (subset of as_linear_surd). Huella-safe if wrappers preserve None-vs-Some behavior; the drift closures (Neg(Phi), Mul-coefficient nth roots) only add decisions, never remove.
verify_reason: Every load-bearing fact checks out, and the finding actually UNDERCOUNTS the duplication. The sign(A+B√n) = sign(B)·sign(B²n−A²) kernel exists in FOUR copies across 3 crates, not three: (1) root_forms.rs:1876 provable_sign_vs_zero tail (1887-1901); (2) solution_set.rs:117 sign_of_linear_surd (byte-equivalent, verified line-by-line); (3) solve_backend_local.rs:1330 cmp_rational_to_quadratic_surd (algebraically reverse(sign_of_linear_surd(a−r,b,n)), verified); plus (4) solve_backend_local.rs:28 linear_surd_sign, a byte-level clone the claim missed, whose own doc (line 22) admits it is 'the same exact surd-sign logic as cas_math::root_forms::provable_sign_vs_zero'. Not deliberate layering: cas_solver_core already depends on cas_math (Cargo.toml:12) and solution_set.rs:113 already delegates to root_forms::as_linear_surd — the local kernels exist only because the raw (p,q,n) kernel is unexported. split_linear_surd (1659, single caller at 2186) and as_linear_surd_expr (2000) confirmed as claimed. Per protocol, confirmed divergence raises severity; P1 stands (drifts observed are spurious-decline/ordering-inconsistency class, not a live wrong answer).
extra_evidence: Fourth copy: crates/cas_solver/src/solve_backend_local.rs:28 fn linear_surd_sign(a,b,n) — identical zero-cases, same-sign shortcut, (b*b*n).cmp(&(a*a)) with reverse() on b.is_negative(); used by inv_trig_arg_provably_out_of_range (line 55) and the TrigThresholdRegion classifier. The proposed fix should fold this copy in too. Mitigating detail for the -φ drift: compare_values (solution_set.rs:84-99) falls back to cas_math::const_sign::const_value_bounds, which handles Phi (const_sign.rs:419 via arbitrary-precision sqrt bounds), so -φ vs a SEPARATED value still orders correctly; only equal/overlapping values (e.g. -φ vs (1−√5)/2) fall to value-blind structural compare — a latent Equal-vs-arbitrary inconsistency rather than a broad mis-ordering.

title: Recursive-descent parser has no depth guard: nested input aborts the process (uncatchable stack overflow)
evidence: parse_expr (parser.rs:720) -> parse_term (641) -> parse_unary (629) -> parse_power (593) -> parse_factorial (568) -> parse_atom (534) -> parse_parens (551) -> parse_expr is mutually recursive with no depth counter (grep for 'depth|recursion|MAX_' in cas_parser/src returns nothing). Verified against ./target/release/cas_cli: input of ~5000 nested '(' (n=2000 OK, n=5000 crashes) and 'sin('*6000 both print 'thread main has overflowed its stack / fatal runtime error: stack overflow, aborting'. Only ~10KB of input, far under the web server's --max-chars 500000. cas_cli/src/commands/eval.rs:67 max_chars limits OUTPUT only; no pre-parse nesting check.
why: A single equation string typed by a user aborts the whole process (SIGABRT). Reachable from the CLI stdin, from web/server.py's eval subprocess (returns a 500 but crashes the worker each call), and critically from the Android FFI: cas_android_ffi/src/lib.rs wraps eval in catch_unwind and its doc claims 'All panics are caught with catch_unwind', but stack overflow aborts and is NOT catchable by catch_unwind, so the Android app crashes. This is a hard blocker for treating the parser as a safe front door for a universality campaign.
fix: Add an explicit recursion-depth counter threaded through the parse_expr/parse_term/parse_unary/parse_atom chain (and the analogous latex_parser.rs chain) that returns a ParseError::Syntax once a bounded depth (e.g. 256) is exceeded, before recursing further. Correct the cas_android_ffi doc comment that claims all panics are caught.
verify_reason: Every load-bearing element reproduces. (1) No depth guard exists: grep for depth/recursion/MAX_ across crates/cas_parser/src returns nothing, and the mutual recursion chain is real — parse_expr (parser.rs:720) → parse_term (641) → parse_unary (629) → parse_power (593) → parse_factorial (568) → parse_atom (534) → parse_parens (defined at 346, dispatched from parse_atom at 551) → parse_expr, all plain recursive nom combinators with no counter. (2) Crash reproduced against ./target/release/cas_cli (built today, HEAD ab5587aa1): 2000 nested '(' returns exit 0 / 'x'; 5000 nested '(' aborts with exit 134 (SIGABRT), 'thread main has overflowed its stack / fatal runtime error: stack overflow, aborting'; 'sin('×6000 likewise exit 134. Passing the web server's own flag --max-chars 500000 does not help — cli_args.rs:297 documents max_chars as 'Maximum characters in result output (wire output only, truncates if larger)', i.e. output truncation, no input-nesting check anywhere pre-parse. (3) Android FFI reachability traced end-to-end: cas_android_ffi/src/lib.rs evalWire (line 67 catch_unwind) → eval_core → cas_solver::wire::eval_str_to_wire (wire/eval.rs:35) → prepare_stateless_eval_request (wire/eval/prepare.rs:16) → build_prepared_eval_request_for_input (eval_input/build.rs:21) → statement::build_statement_request → cas_parser (eval_input/build/statement.rs:13). Rust stack overflow is a guard-page abort, not an unwind, so catch_unwind cannot intercept it and the lib.rs:7 doc claim 'All panics are caught with catch_unwind to prevent crashes crossing FFI' is violated in effect: a user-typed ~10KB string aborts the whole Android app process. P1 is appropriate: trivially reachable from every front door of a parser that the safety docs present as crash-proof, uncatchable, foundation-level.
extra_evidence: One overstated detail that does not change the verdict: on the web path the Python server itself survives — web/server.py:916 runs cas_cli as a per-request subprocess and server.py:924-930 catches the non-zero exit (134) and returns an {"ok": false} error JSON, so it is a graceful per-request failure, not a crashed web worker. The severity carriers are the direct CLI (hard SIGABRT, session lost) and especially the in-process Android FFI (whole app aborts despite the documented catch_unwind guarantee). Also a trivial nit in the evidence: parse_parens is defined at parser.rs:346; line 551 is the parse_atom match arm that dispatches to it. The proposed fix (threaded depth counter returning ParseError, mirrored in latex_parser.rs, plus correcting the cas_android_ffi doc comment) is sound; latex_parser.rs equally lacks any depth/recursion guard per the same grep.

title: Unbounded, budget-ignoring factorial-ratio step expansion (resource-exhaustion DoS)
evidence: rebuild_consecutive_factorial_ratio_expansion computes let gap = num_offset - den_offset (8228) from user-controlled factorial offsets, guards only if gap <= 0 (8229-8231), then does Vec::with_capacity(gap as usize) (8233) and for shift in (1..=gap).rev() (8234) building an ExprId per iteration via rebuild_expr_with_offset_local. num_offset/den_offset come from small_integer() which accepts any i64. No upper bound and no deadline check inside the loop. Verified: echo '(n + 1099511627776)!/n!' | cas_cli eval --steps on hangs >30s (exit 124), and STILL hangs >20s with --time-budget-ms 2000 (exit 124), so the cooperative time budget does not bound it.
why: With --steps on (the default the web servers pass: server.py call_cas_cli steps_on=True), a short user expression pins a CPU/allocates unboundedly for the full hard timeout, tying up a web worker per request; on the Android FFI there is no subprocess timeout so the app hangs. Also num_offset - den_offset can itself overflow i64 (debug panic / release wrap). Directly blocks safely exposing the didactic factorial path.
fix: Cap gap to a small maximum (e.g. return None when gap exceeds ~64) before allocating/looping, use checked_sub for num_offset - den_offset, and/or poll the engine deadline inside the loop.
verify_reason: The DoS is real and reproduced (echo-style repro hangs: exit 124 at 20s; --time-budget-ms 2000 still hangs 15s+, so the cooperative budget does not bound the loop; didactic substeps confirmed live under --steps on). However the finding mis-attributes the choke point: the identical unbounded gap loop exists in the core simplifier at crates/cas_math/src/number_theory_support.rs:484-505 (try_rewrite_consecutive_factorial_ratio_expr), which fires BEFORE any didactic code and hangs even with --steps off (verified: exit 124). The didactic function at focused_rule_substeps.rs:8228 is a downstream duplicate only reachable after the engine loop completes, so the proposed fix at that file alone would fix nothing — the repro would hang identically. Severity P1 stands (Android FFI is in-process with only the cooperative budget, which this loop ignores; web workers are pinned up to the 60s hard timeout per ~25-char request), but the fix must land in the engine-side function, with the didactic twin capped as defense-in-depth.
extra_evidence: Verified with target/release/cas_cli (built 2026-07-02, HEAD ab5587aa1): (1) eval '(n + 1099511627776)!/n!' --steps on → exit 124 (timeout 20s); (2) same with --steps off → exit 124, proving the hang is in the engine rule, not the didactic layer; (3) --steps on --time-budget-ms 2000 → exit 124 at 15s, proving budget-ignoring; (4) gap=400000 completes in 0.5s both modes (linear cost, ~15 days extrapolated for the 2^40 repro). Primary site: crates/cas_math/src/number_theory_support.rs:484 'let gap = num_offset - den_offset;' guarded only by 'gap <= 0', then Vec::with_capacity(gap as usize) + for shift in 1..=gap — same shape as the claimed didactic site. It is invoked from the simplifier rule 'Consecutive Factorial Ratio' (crates/cas_engine/src/rules/number_theory.rs:12-16) and from crates/cas_engine/src/rules/arithmetic.rs:19859. The didactic generator is dispatched only on that rule name (focused_rule_substeps.rs:368), i.e., only after the engine loop succeeded, so capping gap in the engine function also makes the didactic site unreachable with large gaps; still mirror the cap there since it independently recomputes the expansion twice (build_ + rebuild_, lines 8119/8139) plus O(gap) rendering. The i64-overflow sub-claim is real but minor: num_offset - den_offset can overflow only with ~2^63-scale offsets (debug panic, release wrap → likely gap<=0 → None); also rebuild_expr_with_offset_local's checked_abs().expect() panics on offset == i64::MIN. Corrected fix: cap gap (e.g. <=64) and/or poll the deadline in try_rewrite_consecutive_factorial_ratio_expr (number_theory_support.rs:484), use checked_sub; apply the same cap to the focused_rule_substeps.rs:8228 twin.

title: Module-wide #![allow(dead_code)] hides a superseded ~260-line Pythagorean-preservation cluster plus commented-out debug code
evidence: Line 1: `#![allow(dead_code)]` over the whole 452-line module. Whole-workspace word-boundary grep: `should_preserve_trig_function` (line 263) has 0 refs anywhere (not even tests); `collect_add_chain` (line 15) only self-recursive refs; `is_sec_tan_pattern` (185), `is_csc_cot_pattern` (197), `is_pythagorean_with_constant` (209), `expr_contains`, `matches_pattern_containing`, `is_part_of_pythagorean_pattern` (251) are reachable only from the file's own #[cfg(test)] mod. Only 5 functions are production-live: `try_extract_all_negative_sum` (trig_core_identity_support.rs:10) and `is_{sec,tan,csc,cot}_squared` (pattern_scanner.rs:8-10). BUT the claim contains a material factual error that makes the proposed fix build-breaking: collect_add_chain is NOT dead. Deleting it as proposed would break a live trig-parity rewrite path. Severity demoted: this is unreachable hygiene code with a working live replacement, no rule-registry entry, and no demonstrated divergence hazard — maintenance cost only.
extra_evidence: Corrected fix list: KEEP collect_add_chain (live helper of try_extract_all_negative_sum) and the four is_*_squared helpers (imported by pattern_scanner.rs:8-9). DELETE should_preserve_trig_function (lines 261-361, including all commented println blocks), is_sec_tan_pattern, is_csc_cot_pattern, is_pythagorean_with_constant, expr_contains, matches_pattern_containing, is_part_of_pythagorean_pattern, args_equal (line 9, only used by the deleted pattern fns), and the tests that exercise only deleted fns (test_is_sec_tan_pattern, test_is_pythagorean_with_constant, test_is_part_of_pythagorean_pattern). title: Duplicate division-distribution planner: guard_support copy is test-only, superseded by the live distribution_division_support implementation
evidence: `try_rewrite_distribute_division_when_simplifying_expr` (lines 412-463) is a near line-for-line duplicate of `try_rewrite_div_distribution_simplifying_expr` (distribution_division_support.rs:26-70): same AddView loop, same `estimate_division_distribution_simplification_reduction` heuristic, same `new_complexity > old_complexity + total_reduction` gate. verify_reason: Core finding CONFIRMED with exact line numbers: the division-distribution planner cluster in distribution_guard_support.rs (DivisionDistributionRewrite :399, DistributionRewritePlan :405, try_rewrite_distribute_division_when_simplifying_expr :412-463, try_plan_distribution_rewrite_expr :467-488) is referenced only by the file's own #[cfg(test)] mod (:498-499, :583, :683) — verified by whole-tree grep over all file types (no macro name-strings, no scripts, no re-export consumers). The live twin try_rewrite_div_distribution_simplifying_expr (distribution_division_support.rs:25-70) is production code (cas_engine/src/rules/polynomial/mod.rs:30, cas_solver/src/derive/fractions.rs:7) and is semantically identical (as_div == the same Div pattern match; the reduction heuristic is literally shared via import, so only the driver loop + complexity gate are duplicated; only delta is the dead copy's desc field). So the mul-planner trio (classify/try_plan_mul_distribution_candidate, build_mul_distribution_expr, AdditiveOperator, MulDistributionCandidate) can also be deleted or must be justified by its own in-file tests — the parenthetical "they have real external callers" in the proposed fix is false. title: Dead budget-instrumentation variants mul_with_stats/div_exact_with_stats contradict BUDGET_POLICY.md, which documents them as the unified poly-op charging hooks

title: Structural blind spot: 332 of 1434 pub fns (23%) have zero references outside their defining file, so rustc's dead_code lint sees nothing in this crate
evidence: Whole-workspace token scan (includes string literals, so macro-name dispatch is counted): 1434 `pub fn` in cas_math; 332 have 0 refs in any other file; 15 have 0 refs anywhere (finding above); heaviest pub-but-file-local counts: number_theory_support.rs 33, limits_support.rs 21, summation_support.rs 21, div_expand_cancel_support.rs 15, trig_canonicalization_support.rs 14, trig_contraction_support.rs 14. why: Every dead item found in this audit was invisible to clippy -D warnings solely because of `pub`; without visibility discipline the universality campaign will keep accreting unreachable helpers in the god files, and future audits must re-do whole-workspace scans to find them.
